narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 49.7% chance that you would not pass—right on the edge, but enough to tip the decision against admission. This prediction draws from your academic record, like your undergraduate GPA of 3.20 and LSAT score of 37, alongside details such as your Year 1 and Year 3 school deciles, family income level, and plans to study full-time. The model zeroed in on three key factors that shaped this outcome, with your Year 3 decile ranking of 2 standing out as the strongest influence, pushing the prediction heavily toward expecting a bar failure because it signals performance well below the typical top-third standing of students who pass. In contrast, your undergraduate GPA, slightly under the 3.24 average for bar passers, worked in your favor by nudging the odds toward success, much like your LSAT score—which is nearly spot-on with the 37.27 average—provided a balancing positive pull. While your high family income bracket aligns with a smaller but successful subset of passers and your full-time enrollment matches the norm for those who succeed, these didn't outweigh the drag from that lower Year 3 decile. Compared to admitted students, who typically rank in the 6th decile or higher by Year 3 and maintain stronger consistent academic momentum, your profile showed a dip that the model flagged as a high-risk pattern for bar struggles. It's understandable to feel disappointed, especially with your solid LSAT and GPA holding up well against averages, but this highlights how later-year performance often carries the most weight in these predictions. To move forward, consider retaking courses or targeted tutoring to boost your decile rankings, perhaps exploring part-time options if full-time demands proved challenging before. Building a stronger upward trajectory in your final years could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. You might also gather letters from professors attesting to your potential or seek advice from bar prep programs tailored to students with similar starting points. With focused effort on those academic deciles, you're well-positioned to reapply stronger next cycle.
